# Your Guide to Seamless Legal Tech Implementation: Part 3 — Change Management

**By Onit**

|

April 4, 2023

7 min read

Enterprise Legal Management

*Part three of a three-part “Guide to Seamless Legal Tech Implementation” Parts one and two are below.*

[*Part 1 – Scoping the project before buying or building the technology*](https://onitprostg.wpengine.com/?p=21233)  
[*Part 2 – Ensuring the internal and external project stakeholders are on-board*](https://onitprostg.wpengine.com/?p=21234)

Change management is essential for successful legal tech implementation. It is often understated or not fully appreciated, sometimes seen as an “add-on” to the project’s more technical aspects. Those who underestimate the importance of change management during implementation often fail to achieve the desired business outcomes.

Routine tasks will change, and with that comes an inevitable learning curve. For example, with e-billing, manual invoice checks and approvals are replaced by automated rules-based processing. Change also brings more exposure to and interaction with business processes and data. Humans are wired to resist change, with numerous studies showing a strong preference for items and processes that have been around longer, even when maintaining the status quo is irrational. Changing takes conscious effort to learn something new, so communication is vital when presenting changes and benefits. Often the “why” and the “how” are not communicated company wide. Why has the organization decided to do this, and how will it benefit the organization and those that work there?

Unless everyone has a clear vision and understanding of the potential benefits, it can be difficult to institute change management. Change management includes guiding, educating, encouraging, and supporting the organization through this transition period. Complexity and scope define change management strategy for a [software project](https://onitprostg.wpengine.com/?p=21218). Let’s break this down into six key sections.

## Sponsorship

Active sponsorship for change at a senior executive level is vital to success. This sponsorship must be active and visible to the project team and all stakeholders. In many cases, the sponsor will set the project’s strategic direction and drive the program forward. They will judge any conflicts that may arise and act as an “honest broker” in issue resolution to move the legal tech implementation forward. The sponsor will also be the primary source of high-level project communications and be the figurehead of any key announcements to the broader user population.

## Stakeholder Buy-In

As we identified in the previous blog, it is essential to identify the key project stakeholders and stakeholder groups affected directly or indirectly. Some of these groups or individuals may be fully on board with the program and may require little management in expectations. If using a phased approach, start with this group and use them as a case study to communicate their achievements because of the change. It’s also better that any issues happen with a keen sub-group than the whole company.

Some likely key stakeholders may have other priorities or may be resistant to the change that a major project will bring about. Managing this resistance is very important if the project is to succeed, and you can use many tools and techniques to overcome it. This can range from the obvious, such as education and communication strategies, to the more extreme measures, including negotiation and bargaining. A last resort – coercion – will likely be counter-productive in the long term.

## Involvement

Legal tech implementation and changing key processes will inevitably result in role and responsibility changes for some of the organization’s employees. The likely impact will depend on the business process level and operational change undertaken. As part of the planning phase, it is vital to engage and involve team members whose day-to-day tasks will change and capture the key business processes, both current and new, including reasons for change. These are likely the same users who provided requirements for the scoping document. This will help to ensure only necessary changes are made, not change for change’s sake. Also, capture their perceived negatives and positives, as you are unlikely to have considered everything; this helps with the implementation of legal tech and communication later. This analysis exercise will provide valuable information to the project team and the basis for discussions on potentially sensitive job reassignments undertaken as part of the implementation. With e-billing, most changes involve freeing legal teams of administrative burden to focus on high-value work, so the organization should positively receive these changes.

## Impact

It may seem obvious, but it’s not always the case that identifying and measuring the benefits to be achieved is an essential stage in any legal project implementation. The structures and processes need successful implementation to help realize those benefits. The organization may not identify these benefits before implementing change but capturing them is essential. Benefits realization will extend beyond the implementation timescale, as benefits are often not realized until well after the go-live date.

## Communication

On most projects, the first communication task undertaken by the legal change management team is the project launch announcement. Even though this may seem to have come from the project sponsor, the actual drafting will likely be by the project team itself. At the project launch, the team should inform the broader company about the project, its objectives, the expected benefits, key milestones, and members of the project team. There are many ways to transmit communications through an organization, which will often depend on the culture within the company. For example, it could be that the company’s intranet is a repository for updates and general project news, as well as other means – newsletters, departmental meetings, workshops, e-mail, etc.

It is essential to have a communication plan within the wider project plan, identifying formal information-related events as the project unfolds. As well as progress updates, inform employees of new process designs and changes. The more widely you communicate potential changes, the more likely people will understand, rationalize the benefits, and be open and accepting. It will also help with identifying possible project early adopters, who will prove invaluable when it comes to reinforcing the changes among their peers.

## Readiness

Training is often left to the end of the project plan but is critical in preparing the company for a change. The project team should identify essential elements such as the approach to training, the delivery styles to be deployed, the level and nature of end-user help documentation, and how the team will measure the training program’s success. Training courses and documentation development must fully incorporate the business process information alongside the change messages and reasons for change. Courses and documents must match the depth of knowledge different groups of individuals need, from overviews to power-user level.

The organization will need help desk services, hand-holding and intensive one-to-one assistance, particularly in the immediate post-go-live period. Some [software vendors](https://onitprostg.wpengine.com/?p=21229) provide this, but you may need to arrange it yourself, and your law firms will need support too. This investment will not only support the go-live process but will also serve as a knowledge base for new joiner training programs and possible future phases of implementation.

Related to training is the usability of the software you purchase or build. Do users need to learn a new interface? Is there another login to remember? How easy to understand is it? Many projects fail because users cannot get to grips with the system and find it easier to stick with the old process.

Change, in its broadest sense, is constant in organizations today and can be driven by many different forces, including customers, markets, and technology. Yet research shows that many change initiatives fail to accomplish their intended outcomes and may even limit the potential of an organization and its people.

The consequences of not managing change effectively can be devastating and long-lasting, so all company-wide stakeholders must understand the potential issues and equip themselves with techniques to support change-management initiatives.

Top 3 change management methodologies for legal innovation teams: driving tech adoption
=======================================================================================

![Image 19](https://www.legatics.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/RE-checklist-1200x800-3-1024x683.jpg)

Tags: [adoption](https://www.legatics.com/tag/adoption/), [change management](https://www.legatics.com/tag/change-management/) |  Share Article 

[](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.legatics.com%2Fchange-management-methodologies-for-legal-innovation-teams-driving-tech-adoption%2F "Facebook")[](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?text=Top%203%20change%20management%20methodologies%20for%20legal%20innovation%20teams%3A%20driving%20tech%20adoption&url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.legatics.com%2Fchange-management-methodologies-for-legal-innovation-teams-driving-tech-adoption%2F "X")[](https://www.linkedin.com/sharing/share-offsite/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.legatics.com%2Fchange-management-methodologies-for-legal-innovation-teams-driving-tech-adoption%2F "Linkedin")[](https://www.legatics.com/change-management-methodologies-for-legal-innovation-teams-driving-tech-adoption/ "More")

[Sophie Cramer](https://www.legatics.com/author/sophie-cramer/)

Law firms are increasingly embracing technological solutions like Legatics to enhance efficiency, and improve the client and lawyer experience on deals. However, as we know from our experience at Legatics, implementing new technology is only half the battle. The real challenge lies in ensuring widespread adoption and integration into daily workflows. This blog post explores three proven change management methodologies that innovation teams can leverage to facilitate smooth technology implementation and maximize user adoption. See our blog post on [why change management is so important for legal tech implementation.](https://www.legatics.com/importance-of-change-management/)

1. Kotter’s 8-Step Change Model
-------------------------------

John Kotter’s 8-Step Change Model is a comprehensive framework that can guide legal innovation teams through the entire change process:

1.   **Create a sense of urgency:** Clearly communicate the need for technological change within the firm.
2.   **Build a guiding coalition:** Form a team of influential leaders and stakeholders to champion the change.
3.   **Form a strategic vision:** Develop a clear picture of how the new technology will benefit the firm and its clients.
4.   **Enlist a volunteer army:** Engage a broad base of employees to drive and support the change.
5.   **Enable action by removing barriers:** Identify and address obstacles to adoption, such as outdated processes or resistance to change.
6.   **Generate short-term wins:** Celebrate early successes to build momentum and motivation.
7.   **Sustain acceleration:** Use the momentum from early wins to tackle bigger changes.
8.   **Institute change:** Anchor the new approaches in the firm’s culture to ensure long-term success.

This methodology is particularly effective for large-scale technology implementations that require firm-wide buy-in and cultural shifts.

![Image 20](https://www.legatics.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/Kotters-1.png)
ADKAR Model
-----------

The ADKAR model, developed by Prosci, focuses on the individual’s journey through change. It’s an excellent approach for innovation teams looking to address the human side of technology adoption:

1.   **Awareness:** Ensure that everyone understands why the technological change is necessary.
2.   **Desire:** Foster a willingness to support and participate in the change.
3.   **Knowledge:** Provide information and training on how to change.
4.   **Ability:** Offer support to implement new skills and behaviors.
5.   **Reinforcement:** Create mechanisms to sustain the change over time.

This model is particularly useful when dealing with resistance to new technology, as it addresses the personal and emotional aspects of change.

![Image 21](https://www.legatics.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/Adkar.png)
Lean Change Management
----------------------

Lean Change Management, inspired by Agile and Lean startup principles, is an iterative, flexible approach well-suited for the dynamic nature of legal tech implementation:

1.   **Co-create change:** Involve end-users in the planning and implementation process.
2.   **Validate assumptions:** Test ideas and gather feedback before full-scale implementation.
3.   **Experiment and adapt:** Use small-scale pilots to learn and refine the approach.
4.   **Continuous improvement:** Regularly review and adjust the change strategy based on real-time feedback and results.

This methodology is particularly effective for innovation teams working in fast-paced environments where flexibility and rapid iteration are crucial.

![Image 22](https://www.legatics.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/Lean-change-management-1.png)
Conclusion
----------

Successful technology implementation in law firms requires more than just selecting the right tools; it demands a strategic approach to change management. By leveraging these methodologies, innovation teams can significantly improve adoption rates, minimize resistance, and maximize the return on technology investments.

The key to success lies in choosing the methodology (or combination of methodologies) that best fits your firm’s culture, the specific technology being implemented, and the scale of the change. With the right approach, your innovation team can drive meaningful technological transformation and position your firm at the forefront of legal innovation.

The 2025 Change Management Checklist for Legal Teams

The 2025 Change Management Checklist for Legal Teams
====================================================

May 9, 2025

![Image 2](https://framerusercontent.com/images/BUSf6qRoyv8KQR05Iu4uyNz62J8.jpeg)

Change management in legal departments demands a strategic approach, balancing technology adoption with human considerations. In this checklist, we provide actionable steps to implement effective changes while ensuring data and contract compliance in 2025’s rapidly evolving legal environment.

Why most legal tech implementations fail
----------------------------------------

According to research by the [Association of Corporate Counsel (ACC)](https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2025/01/28/3016565/25851/en/Risk-Compliance-Data-Privacy-and-Regulatory-Changes-Named-Top-Concerns-for-Global-Chief-Legal-Officers.html), 44% of Chief Legal Officers plan to adopt new legal technology to improve efficiency this year, with [contract management software](https://www.concord.app/contract-management-software/?utm_campaign=91792579-Guest%20Post%20Awareness&utm_source=Guest%20post&utm_term=contract%20management%20software) topping the list at 62% of planned initiatives. Yet many of these implementations will stumble.

The disconnect? Most legal teams focus exclusively on technology specifications while overlooking the human element. As one legal operations expert notes in [BusyLamp’s implementation guide](https://www.busylamp.com/implementing-successful-legal-technology-change-management/), those who “underestimate the importance of change management during implementation often fail to achieve the desired business outcomes.”

The three critical success factors
----------------------------------

### 1. Executive sponsorship

Every successful [legal contract management software](https://www.concord.app/legal-contract-management-software/?utm_campaign=91792579-Guest%20Post%20Awareness&utm_source=Guest%20post&utm_term=legal%20contract%20managemwent%20software) implementation requires active sponsorship from a senior executive who can:

*   Articulate how the change aligns with broader business objectives

*   Remove obstacles that arise during implementation

*   Provide visible support at key milestones

Robert Mignanelli, Senior VP and Chief Operating Officer of Legal at Pearson, demonstrated this principle when implementing their contract management solution. His visible leadership helped transform the company’s contract processes and earned them multiple industry recognitions, including the ACC Value Champion Award, according to [Onit’s case study](https://www.onit.com/blog/how-to-calculate-roi-for-contract-lifecycle-management-solutions/).

### 2. Clear change narrative

Legal teams need a compelling story about why change is necessary. Your narrative should address:

*   **The problem**: What specific pain points does the current process create?

*   **The solution**: How will the new approach solve these issues?

*   **The benefits**: What concrete improvements will team members experience?

For example, when implementing a new data governance framework to meet GDPR requirements, don’t just emphasize compliance mandates. Instead, explain how streamlined [contract compliance management software](https://www.concord.app/contract-compliance-management-software/?utm_campaign=91792579-Guest%20Post%20Awareness&utm_source=Guest%20post&utm_term=contract%20compliance%20management%20software) will make daily work easier while reducing risk exposure, as recommended in [Alation’s GDPR compliance guide](https://www.alation.com/blog/gdpr-data-compliance-best-practices-2025/).

### 3. Measurable success metrics

Successful change initiatives define concrete metrics to track progress. For legal departments, consider metrics like:

| Metric category | Example KPIs |
| --- | --- |
| Efficiency | Contract cycle time (30-50% reduction target) |
| Risk reduction | Compliance incidents (50-70% reduction target) |
| Cost control | Outside counsel spend (10-30% reduction target) |
| Quality | Contract dispute frequency (30-50% reduction target) |

The 8-step implementation checklist
-----------------------------------

### STEP 1: Assess your current state

✓ Conduct a thorough audit of existing processes, identifying inefficiencies and compliance gaps 

✓ Document your current contract management workflow, noting bottlenecks and risks 

✓ Gather baseline metrics for later comparison

**Pro tip**: Use anonymous surveys to uncover honest feedback about pain points. [Deloitte’s research](https://www2.deloitte.com/us/en/pages/tax/articles/contract-management-lifecycle-insights.html) found that data silos, where contract information is scattered across systems, are the primary obstacle to effective contract management. Implementing a centralized [contract repository software](https://www.concord.app/contract-repository-software/?utm_campaign=91792579-Guest%20Post%20Awareness&utm_source=Guest%20post&utm_term=contract%20repository%20software) can address this challenge.

### STEP 2: Define your data & compliance requirements

✓ Identify all relevant regulations affecting your contracts (GDPR, CCPA, industry-specific) 

✓ Document required contract clauses and compliance controls 

✓ Map data flows within your contract processes

**Pro tip**: In 2025, five new U.S. states will implement consumer privacy laws, each with specific requirements for contract language, according to [Athennian’s compliance guide](https://www.athennian.com/post/top-compliance-challenges-for-general-counsels-in-2025). Build flexibility into your compliance framework to accommodate these evolving demands, especially for organizations in highly regulated industries that need specialized [healthcare contract management software](https://www.concord.app/solutions/healthcare-contract-management-software/?utm_campaign=91792579-Guest%20Post%20Awareness&utm_source=Guest%20post&utm_term=healthcare%20contract%20management%20software).

### STEP 3: Select your implementation team & champions

✓ Include representatives from legal, IT, procurement, and affected business units 

✓ Identify “champions” who will model adoption for peers 

✓ Assign specific responsibilities for implementation phases

**Pro tip**: Research by [Prosci](https://www.prosci.com/blog/successful-change-management-examples-for-inspiration) shows that 45% of organizations leverage networks of change champions to spread benefits and build support across the organization. For procurement-focused implementations, ensure specialists familiar with [procurement contract management software](https://www.concord.app/procurement-contract-management-software/?utm_campaign=91792579-Guest%20Post%20Awareness&utm_source=Guest%20post&utm_term=procurement%20contract%20management%20software) are part of the team.

### STEP 4: Develop your phased implementation plan

✓ Break implementation into manageable phases (3-4 months each) 

✓ Start with a pilot group of motivated users 

✓ Create realistic timelines with buffer for unexpected issues

**Pro tip**: Studies show that phased approaches generate higher satisfaction and adoption rates than “big bang” implementations. [Lawcadia’s implementation guide](https://www.lawcadia.com/blog/successful-change-management/) recommends beginning with a focused user group to develop success stories before wider rollout. The [best contract lifecycle management software](https://www.concord.app/best-contract-lifecycle-management-software/?utm_campaign=91792579-Guest%20Post%20Awareness&utm_source=Guest%20post&utm_term=best%20contract%20lifecycle%20management%20software) platforms offer staged implementation options to support this approach.

### STEP 5: Design your training & communications strategy

✓ Develop role-specific training materials 

✓ Create a regular cadence of communications about project progress 

✓ Establish feedback channels for questions and concerns

**Pro tip**: Customize training for different user groups. Contract specialists need in-depth system knowledge, while occasional users need quick reference guides for their specific tasks, as highlighted in [Thomson Reuters’ legal department roadmap](https://www.wolterskluwer.com/en-gb/expert-insights/the-2024-roadmap-for-corporate-legal-departments). Modern [contract lifecycle management software](https://www.concord.app/contract-lifecycle-management-software/?utm_campaign=91792579-Guest%20Post%20Awareness&utm_source=Guest%20post&utm_term=contract%20lifecycle%20management%20software) includes role-based access and interfaces to support different user needs.

### STEP 6: Implement technology with close support

✓ Provide extra support during the first 2-3 weeks after launch 

✓ Conduct daily stand-ups to address issues quickly 

✓ Document common problems and solutions for future reference

**Pro tip**: According to implementation experts, having dedicated support resources available during the initial weeks significantly improves adoption rates and reduces resistance, as noted in [Trackado’s contract management best practices](https://www.trackado.com/blog/7-contract-management-best-practices-for-2025/). Consider how [contract automation software](https://www.concord.app/contract-automation-software/?utm_campaign=91792579-Guest%20Post%20Awareness&utm_source=Guest%20post&utm_term=contract%20automation%20software) can streamline common workflows during this critical period.

### STEP 7: Measure results & celebrate wins

✓ Track key metrics from Step 1 baseline 

✓ Recognize and publicize early success stories 

✓ Address any shortfalls transparently with action plans

**Pro tip**: Research shows that celebrating early wins builds momentum. Share specific examples, like “Team reduced contract approval time from 14 days to 3 days in the first month.” Use [contract analytics software](https://www.concord.app/contract-analytics-software/?utm_campaign=91792579-Guest%20Post%20Awareness&utm_source=Guest%20post&utm_term=contract%20analytics%20software) to provide concrete data on improvements and ROI.

### STEP 8: Refine & optimize

✓ Conduct 30/60/90 day reviews to identify improvement opportunities 

✓ Implement system enhancements based on user feedback 

✓ Expand functionality as users become proficient

**Pro tip**: According to [Gartner’s legal technology trends report](https://www.gartner.com/smarterwithgartner/5-legal-technology-trends-changing-in-house-legal-departments), legal departments that incorporate user feedback into ongoing optimization achieve higher satisfaction rates than those who consider implementation complete at launch. Keep track of [contract management software pricing](https://www.concord.app/contract-management-software/pricing-details/?utm_campaign=91792579-Guest%20Post%20Awareness&utm_source=Guest%20post&utm_term=contract%20management%20software%20pricing) to ensure your solution remains cost-effective as you scale.

Real-world success story: Global financial institution
------------------------------------------------------

A leading financial services company needed to transform their contract management processes to address growing regulatory demands and inefficient workflows. Their legal department implemented a structured change management approach that included:

*   Designating “legal tech ambassadors” within each practice area

*   Creating a detailed communication plan with weekly updates

*   Developing customized training paths for different user roles

*   Establishing clear metrics to track success

The results were impressive: 42% reduction in contract cycle time, 68% decrease in compliance incidents, and significantly improved ability to respond to regulatory audits, according to a case study in [Bloomberg Law’s legal trends report](https://news.bloomberglaw.com/legal-ops-and-tech/four-trends-for-corporate-legal-departments-to-watch-in-2025).

Avoiding common pitfalls
------------------------

As you implement your change management plan, watch for these common warning signs:

*   **Resistance indicators**: Declining meeting attendance, minimal participation, or subtle sabotage

*   **Scope creep**: Expanding requirements beyond the original project boundaries

*   **Executive disengagement**: Sponsors becoming less visible as implementation progresses

*   **Communication gaps**: Teams operating with inconsistent information about the project

If you spot these signs, pause to address the underlying issues before proceeding. Small course corrections early prevent major problems later.

Frequently asked questions about legal change management
--------------------------------------------------------

How long should our legal team expect a contract management implementation to take?

Implementation timelines vary based on complexity and organizational size, but effective planning typically follows this pattern:

*   Initial assessment and planning: 2-4 weeks

*   System configuration and data migration: 4-8 weeks

*   Pilot testing and refinement: 2-4 weeks

*   Training and full rollout: 2-6 weeks

Modern platforms like [Concord’s contract management software](https://www.concord.app/contract-management-software/?utm_campaign=91792579-Guest%20Post%20Awareness&utm_source=Guest%20post&utm_term=contract%20management%20software) can significantly accelerate this timeline, with some implementations completed in as little as 24 hours compared to 6+ months for legacy systems.

How can we ensure our contract data remains compliant with evolving privacy regulations?

Ensuring ongoing compliance requires a multi-faceted approach:

*   Implement regular compliance audits against current regulations

*   Use [contract compliance management software](https://www.concord.app/contract-compliance-management-software/?utm_campaign=91792579-Guest%20Post%20Awareness&utm_source=Guest%20post&utm_term=contract%20compliance%20management%20software) with automated alerts for potential issues

*   Establish a regulatory monitoring process to track changes to relevant laws

*   Create standardized clauses that can be quickly updated across all contracts

*   Maintain clear data inventory and mapping to track what information is stored where

This structured approach helps legal teams stay ahead of regulatory changes while maintaining efficient operations.

What’s the most effective way to overcome resistance to new technology from seasoned attorneys?

Overcoming resistance requires understanding the unique concerns of experienced legal professionals:

*   Focus on specific pain points the technology solves in their daily workflow

*   Provide customized training that acknowledges their expertise while building new skills

*   Identify respected peers who can serve as champions and demonstrate the benefits

*   Create a staged adoption approach that allows for gradual skill development

*   Regularly collect and visibly act on their feedback to improve the implementation

Remember that resistance often stems from legitimate concerns about quality and professional standards, not just aversion to change.

How do we measure ROI for our legal technology implementation?

Measuring ROI effectively combines quantitative and qualitative metrics:

*   Time savings: Compare process cycle times before and after implementation

*   Cost reduction: Calculate savings from reduced outside counsel spend and operational efficiency

*   Risk mitigation: Track reduction in compliance incidents and contract disputes

*   User satisfaction: Measure team productivity and satisfaction improvements

*   Strategic impact: Assess improved decision-making through better data access

For a comprehensive ROI assessment, check out [Concord’s pricing and ROI information](https://www.concord.app/contract-management-software/pricing-details/?utm_campaign=91792579-Guest%20Post%20Awareness&utm_source=Guest%20post&utm_term=contract%20management%20software%20pricing) to understand potential returns for your specific organization.

What’s the best approach for training different stakeholders during implementation?

Effective training strategies vary by stakeholder group:

*   **Legal team members:** Comprehensive training on all system functions and compliance controls

*   **Business users:** Focused training on self-service functions and request processes

*   **Executive sponsors:** High-level dashboards and reporting capabilities

*   **IT support:** Technical configuration, integration points, and troubleshooting

Use a mix of training formats (live sessions, recorded tutorials, reference guides) and consider implementing [contract automation solutions](https://www.concord.app/contract-automation-software/?utm_campaign=91792579-Guest%20Post%20Awareness&utm_source=Guest%20post&utm_term=contract%20automation%20software) that offer intuitive interfaces requiring minimal training.

Conclusion: A balanced approach
-------------------------------

Effective change management in legal departments balances technical implementation with the human elements of transition. By following this checklist and maintaining focus on both compliance requirements and team adoption, your legal department can successfully navigate even complex technology transformations.

Remember that change is not a one-time event but an ongoing process. The most successful legal departments build change capability as a core competency, enabling them to continuously adapt to evolving regulatory requirements and business needs.

The steps taken by law firms to engage their change management process to Plan, Build and Deploy new legal tech solutions are critical

Change management is like a prism — depending on one’s frame of reference, it can mean different things. To technologists, the term often applies to strong development and operational practices; while those in legal generally relate change management to process examination, training, and implementation refinements.

Regardless of your focus, effective change management is critical to a successful digital transformation. So, how can we demystify change management for various members of the legal profession and provide a framework for those in disparate roles to communicate effectively?

One way might be to examine a case study. Let’s imagine a need where a law firm or legal software company desired to build a matter management system for a client whose legal issues are truly unique, thus requiring either significant modifications to an off-the-shelf software product or a customized software build.

As we embark on this journey, we will identify three main phases of our case study: _Plan, Build_ and _Deploy_. We will also define the key change management aspects of each phase as we create a customized matter management platform.

**Plan**
--------

**Nadine Ezzie:** Ken, from your viewpoint on the technical side of the shop, what are the key change management points to be addressed during the planning phase of a legal tech build out?

**Kenneth Jones:**The initial phase of a project is one where business requirements are defined. Documenting these in a manner both technologists and legal professionals can comprehend is critical. Defining other essential elements of a project — such as the need to migrate over legacy data, reporting, data interfaces, and necessary workflow — is also vital at this stage.

Defining project scope, deliverables, timing (often in stages), and cost are elements of this project phase as well. Specifically as it relates to matter management, defining data points needed in areas such as legal venue, plaintiff information and allegations, the tracking of matter milestones and key documents, and company specific facilities and hierarchies required for reporting are all part of the mix.

In your legal experience, Nadine, what should happen next?

**Nadine Ezzie:** I find this period to be the most critical when ensuring a successful legal tech transformation or adoption. Attorneys, by our nature, are trained to mitigate risk where possible. Persuading us to change our ways is not easy, but also not impossible. Communication and transparency is key.

Communicating early not only about the scope of the project but also about how it ties into the overall business objectives of the firm or organization can foster greater adoption. I also advise tapping a _legal champion_ of the project. This is someone from the legal team, usually mid- to senior-level, who is open to digital transformation, understands the value it can bring, and will help champion the project to other team members. This last part is critical as these are the users who will actually use the system and enter the relevant data (such as matters, report creation, processing tasks, etc.)

Having your ultimate end-users not only understand the value proposition of the project but also appreciate how it will improve their ability to perform their roles can decrease any sentiments of threat or reluctance to change. Remember, the less surprises, the better.

Defining end goals for a matter management project — for example, lowering the overall cost of a litigation by x% per year, or reducing the average settlement by y% — also are excellent approaches for defining the parameters of a large-scale project.

**Build**
---------

**Nadine Ezzie:** Now, it’s time to build the solution — so, we’re in your wheelhouse, Ken. What change management considerations should be kept in mind during this phase?

**Kenneth Jones:**One vital element in this phase is transforming business requirements into a data model with functional specifications. One best practice during the build phase is to construct the new system, in short-cycle build phases often known as _chewable bites_, and offer frequent visibility to end-users in order to solicit incremental feedback.

Sharing system mockups is a great way to ask users if all the data points associated with a matter are being tracked or if reports created by the system will meet the needs of the client. In our example, this might mean sharing the matter-entry process flow and field list with users prior to moving on to reporting needs to ensure that the required litigation data points are integrated into the system.

And speaking of reporting, reviewing what is created with end-users for purposes like projecting legal costs for future years, filing insurance claims, or assisting with outside counsel evaluation and review processes in an iterative manner makes good sense, especially as reports are built. Indeed, it’s important to review not only the report content, but also the format (PDF, Excel, etc.) and the ability to export or transfer data to other systems. It’s also critical to get this feedback from those in various job functions, such as legal professionals, attorneys, or executives rather than just the technologists.

In the technical area, change management in the build phase also should include code repositories, reviewing code changes, and a formal test plan to better implement system changes.

**Deploy**
----------

**Nadine Ezzie:** This is the phase we’ve all been waiting for. I find that deployment in phases works best for increased adoption. I work with teams to identify which part of their existing process will be modified first, and then set a time frame for training and questions. Wash. Rinse. Repeat.

End-users need to know they have an advocate and support system during this time of deployment that can relate to this time of change. It’s also important to tailor messages to different workers through the process chain. For example, those in legal operations who enter and maintain data would get a different message than those in leadership roles who probably never log into a system but use data reports to project litigation impact and cost.

Well-designed and user-friendly support materials help tremendously as well. Make it so these materials are easily accessible so end-users can refer to them easily once live training support has ended.

**Kenneth Jones:**Clearly, a significant element of deployment is training end-users. They need to know how to address client questions about matters, efficient data update strategies, generating litigation reports, etc. On the tech front, using issue-tracking with tagging to categorize development needs and writing technical documentation are strong practices to follow.

**Conclusion**
--------------

Change management is an intricate process with many flavors — those coordinated by attorneys, legal professionals, or core technology professionals will likely show those differences.

The better those in the legal profession do, the more likely the result of our case study will be improved outcomes and lower costs that’s powered by the new technology which was successfully adopted, used, and even appreciated.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Recognize that change can provoke anxiety among staff; proactively identify potential pushback and develop strategies to address concerns, fostering a smoother transition.
Design training programs tailored to different user roles, ensuring everyone understands the technology and its benefits, thus enhancing confidence and competence in using new tools.
Identify advocates within the organization who can promote the technology, share success stories, and provide peer support to encourage broader acceptance and enthusiasm.
Create an inclusive environment where feedback is valued, encouraging team members to share insights and work together, which enhances adoption and innovation.
Continuously assess the effectiveness of change management initiatives, gathering data and feedback to refine approaches and improve user adoption over time.
